Okay, what are one-on-ones? This is a very broad open-ended question, but I would like to explain to you what these are exactly, so you understand how to do them properly. Okay, this is a one-hour session you have each week with each of your direct reports, and the purpose is to develop a strong relationship, connect on pressing issues, and help your direct report in the goals they wish to achieve. And so this is not a session where you review tasks. You're not reviewing project deadlines or things like that. This is a session for them to basically ask you for whatever support they need for you to listen. Okay, so your direct report should be doing most of the talking. So an example, and this is again something that I would put into the calendar invite. Okay, what's the purpose of this? Develop a strong relationship, connect on pressing issues, help direct report in goals they wish to achieve. Note, these are insanely different depending on what that person needs. Okay, there's a lot of factors that dictate the best way to structure your meetings for success, including the emotional needs of the person you manage, your relationship with them, and the teammates or team members experiencing confidence level at the point in time. Okay, so these are fairly open-ended questions, and they really just allow you to have some dedicated time to listening to that person and giving them your undivided attention. So never be answering emails, always have your phone off, don't even think about checking your phone. That is the most disrespectful thing you could ever do during a one-on-one. And ask that the same of them, but you can't ask it of them unless you're doing it. How often do you do these? Once per week. Some people like to say they only want to do them every two weeks. Every two weeks is irrelevant. Okay, you are connecting every two weeks. There is never, you cannot identify issues fast enough half the time. So once a week is already like, just do not do bi-weekly. Do every week, okay? How often do you do them? 30 minutes to one hour. I start with one hour with everybody, and then I let them dictate. So you are always going to let that person dictate. They are the only one who could ever cancel a meeting, and they are also the only one that could ever make a meeting shorter or longer. It's always up to them. You are serving your direct reports. Okay, this meeting is not for you. It doesn't matter if you're bored. It doesn't matter if you don't feel like doing it. You are serving them. That's all you have to do. One hour a week. That is it. Most people never get that. Okay, so just understand where you're going to do this. You could do it in the gym. However, if it's really busy in there or you don't have an office area, I suggest doing a coffee shop, taking a walk outside, anywhere that you two feel comfortable doing so. Right? What's the agenda? Okay, first you're going to kind of break the ice and just be like, so what do you, you know, what, what have you had on your plate this week? Where is your, your focus at? They're going to kind of answer. It's going to be kind of like whatever. That's just what's used to break the ice. Okay. Then you're going to go into, okay, well, I want to hear like, what do you feel like you're winning right now? Where do you feel like you're doing really well? Let them answer. May take the entire time. It may take just a minute. Right? Then asking where your current constraints. Okay. So they're going to say, you know, I'm having a really hard time with the abandoned hope sale. Like, you know, I'm really struggling. I'm not able to close them any people. I don't know what's wrong with me. Right? And so then that leads into where you get to say, where can I help? Where do you need me to support you? Okay. And at first it might take you offering and giving examples of how you can support. Okay. But it might turn into then they know what that it's okay to ask you for help. All right. I can't tell you how much people value these if done well. If you're not going through a task list and you're truly just dedicating that time to that person and saying, how can I help you become better at your job and ultimately more valuable as a person in the workforce? Like people value that more than anything. You can have all the, you know, celebrations and outings and events as you want. But like, if you're not dedicating time to your people and showing them that their priority through this, they don't care. They don't give a shit. I have asked people so many times, you know, do you feel like you're doing well? Do you feel that our one-on-ones are helpful? And the answer is always an overwhelmingly, oh my gosh, yes, it is my favorite meeting of the week. Aside from our monthly meeting, like I look forward to my one-on-ones more than anything. I'm so excited every time. Like I wait all week. It's what I look forward to. That's what you want. Okay. It's a place where people can feel safe. And what they also feel is they feel that you are supporting them as not just your employee or teammate, but as a person. And a lot of times I will let someone talk to me about their personal issues. Like, hey, if something's bugging you at home, like is there any way I can help? You know, do you need to take a weekend off? Do you need, oh, you're moving? Do you need to take a couple of days off to make sure you get your moving in order so you're not distracted? Oh, someone's sick. Okay. Now I write that down. Like maybe I can send their family member a card. Okay. It's a chance for you to connect as a human. Like you want to work with people who like you and who you like and who you feel comfortable with and you have relationships with, right? There's always boundaries, but like you need to remember that people are human. They need to talk. Okay. And that's the purpose of these one-on-ones.
